stem: A healthcare study measures average patient recovery rate across 5 hospitals, each containing multiple departments. hospital A has 8 observations with mean 65.8 % (within-hospital SD = 4.1). hospital B has 8 observations with mean 70.2 % (within-hospital SD = 8.3). hospital C has 5 observations with mean 68.8 % (within-hospital SD = 9.6). hospital D has 5 observations with mean 63.3 % (within-hospital SD = 8.6). hospital E has 10 observations with mean 84.9 % (within-hospital SD = 8.6). The between-hospital standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 5.9 and 14.7 %. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true average patient recovery rate for hospital D, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-hospital variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for hospital D: 64.5 % (shrunk from raw 63.3). Range [63.9, 66.0] as between-hospital SD varies from 5.9 to 14.7. Pooled mean: 72.2.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 72.2 % (weighted average of 5 hospital means).
Shrinkage factor for hospital D: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/5). At σ_between=9.8: B = 0.867.
Interval [63.9, 66.0] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (63.3) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (72.2) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
